name: monorepo-migration
topic: A B2B SaaS company (50-person engineering org, 8 teams) is experiencing growing pains with their 35 polyrepos. Cross-repo changes require coordinated PRs across 3-5 repos, dependency versions drift, and shared libraries are copy-pasted. Should they migrate to a monorepo?
key_questions: What is the actual migration cost and risk for 35 repos with active development?
Which problems are best solved by monorepo, and which have simpler solutions (better tooling, stricter dependency management)?
What organizational changes does a monorepo require (CI/CD, code ownership, merge process)?
Is there an incremental path, or is this all-or-nothing?

expert_checklist.item: CI/CD pipeline impact — build times, test blast radius, affected-files analysis
Code ownership model (CODEOWNERS complexity, review bottlenecks)
Dependency management — diamond dependencies, version conflicts, single-version policy
Migration cost and risk — 35 active repos is non-trivial, history preservation, tooling rewrite
Tooling requirements — Bazel, Nx, Turborepo, Rush, or custom build system needed
Git performance at scale — shallow clones, sparse checkout, VFS for Git
Conway's Law — repo structure mirrors org structure; monorepo implies tighter coupling between teams
Merge queue and trunk-based development implications at 50 engineers
IDE and developer experience impact — indexing, search, local build times
Cross-repo refactoring friction — the actual pain point driving this discussion
Release independence and deployment coupling — can teams still deploy independently?
Access control and security boundaries between teams/services
Incremental migration path — not all-or-nothing, phased approach possible
Alternatives to monorepo that solve the same problems (better dependency tooling, shared package registry, meta-repo)

traps.description: Arguing 'Google/Meta use monorepo therefore we should' without acknowledging they built $10M+ custom tooling (Bazel, Buck, Piper)
Claiming monorepo is simpler without addressing CI blast radius, merge contention, and the migration itself
